title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
the model was 85% accurate
it caught zero of the patients who needed it


Open with the hook. A hospital wants to flag patients likely to be readmitted within 30 days so it can intervene. It trains a fancy classifier. It reports 85% accuracy. Deploy it, right? Pause. Then the kicker: the model predicts “no readmission” for every patient. That 85% accuracy is just the base rate. Today’s lecture is why accuracy lies when one class dominates — and what to use instead.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
the readmissions setup


the problem

hospital wants to flag 30-day readmissions
missed cases cost up to $25,000 each to treat
CMS penalizes high readmission rates up to 3% of reimbursements


the data

15% of patients get readmitted
85% don’t
predict “no readmission” for everyone →
85% accuracy



the dumbest possible model beats random, passes QA, and saves zero lives

Why we care: readmissions are expensive both clinically and in penalty dollars. But the base rate is 15% positive, which makes the null-model baseline look like a good model on any accuracy-based evaluation. This is our motivating setup. We’ll fit a real classifier on the same kind of data (Framingham CHD — same 15% base rate), discover the same trap, and build a toolkit of metrics that actually tell us whether the model is doing anything useful.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
today

the mechanics: logistic regression, gradient descent
the evaluation trap: why accuracy lies
threshold choice: no free lunch
what accuracy hides: base rates, subgroups, calibration


Four blocks. First: the mechanics — how do we actually fit a classifier? Second: the evaluation trap we opened with. Third: threshold choice as a cost-benefit decision, not a statistical one. Fourth: three ways a model can “work on paper” while being useless in practice — base rate neglect, subgroup failure, miscalibration.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text: first we need a classifier

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
the outcome is binary
Framingham Heart Study: 4,240 patients, 10-year followup
outcome: TenYearCHD \(\in \{0, 1\}\)

CHD = 1 (positive class) · no CHD = 0 (negative class)


“positive” = the outcome we’re trying to detect, not the desirable one

Our running example for the lecture. Framingham is the landmark cardiovascular study from 1948 that identified cholesterol and blood pressure as risk factors. The outcome we’ll try to predict is whether a patient develops coronary heart disease within ten years. That outcome is binary — 1 if CHD, 0 if not. And about 15% of patients are CHD-positive, same as our readmissions hook. Quick vocabulary stake: the class we try to detect is the “positive” class. The terminology is from signal detection and medical testing, where a positive result is one that detects the condition — it’s bad news for the patient, not good news. That distinction will matter when we talk about sensitivity and specificity.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
section: can we just run linear regression?
text:
can we just run linear regression?


predictions below 0 and above 1 — not valid probabilities

The obvious first attempt: fit OLS to the 0/1 labels. It runs. But the predictions are nonsense — many are negative, some exceed 1. Nothing constrains OLS to produce numbers in [0, 1]. We need a model that lives on the probability scale.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
section: the trick: squeeze the line through a sigmoid
text:
the trick: squeeze the line through a sigmoid


\[p = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}, \quad z = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \cdots + \beta_d x_d\]

Rather than constrain OLS, transform what we want to model. The linear predictor z = β₀ + β₁x₁ + ⋯ can be any real number. Push it through the sigmoid σ(z) = 1/(1+e⁻ᶻ) and the output is forced into (0, 1). The sigmoid is symmetric around zero: z = 0 maps to p = 0.5, big positive z maps toward 1, big negative z maps toward 0. Logistic regression is just linear regression on the log-odds of the outcome, then squashed through σ.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
from probabilities to log-odds



probability \(p\)
odds \(p/(1-p)\)
log-odds




0.01
0.01
\(-4.6\)


0.20
0.25
\(-1.4\)


0.50
1.00
\(\phantom{-}0.0\)


0.80
4.00
\(+1.4\)


0.99
99.0
\(+4.6\)




log-odds range from \(-\infty\) to \(+\infty\) — perfect for a linear model
\[\log\frac{p}{1-p} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \cdots\]

Same math, different way to see it. Odds = p / (1-p), familiar from sports betting. Odds range from 0 to infinity. Take the log and the range opens up to the full real line. That’s what we model with a linear function of x. The log-odds are called the logit, and logistic regression is the claim that the logit is linear in the features.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
logistic regression on age alone


predicted risk climbs with age — we see the lower portion of the S-curve because the base rate is low

Fit logistic regression with just age as the feature so we can plot the whole decision curve. The curve rises from under 5% for the youngest patients to over 30% for the oldest. We’re looking at the lower tail of the S — the full sigmoid would sweep through 0.5 and level off at 1, but the base rate of CHD is low enough that age alone doesn’t get anyone to 50% predicted risk. When we add more features, each patient’s predicted probability depends on nine risk factors, not just age — and no one feature tells the whole story.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
the logistic loss
no closed-form solution — what loss do we minimize?

\[\ell(\beta) = -\big[ y \log(p) + (1-y) \log(1-p) \big]\]


penalizes confident wrong predictions especially hard

Linear regression has the normal equations — a closed-form least-squares solution. Logistic regression doesn’t — the sigmoid makes the loss nonlinear. So what should we optimize? The logistic loss, also called cross-entropy or negative log-likelihood, is the standard choice. If the label is 1 and we predicted p, the loss is -log(p); if the label is 0, it’s -log(1-p). Either way, the loss is small when we’re right with high confidence and large when we’re wrong with high confidence. “Confident wrong” is punished severely — that’s why logistic regression picks coefficients that aren’t overconfident on hard cases.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
squared error vs. logistic loss


a 10% prediction for a true 1? squared error 0.81, logistic loss 2.3

The comparison visually. When the prediction is near 1 (correct, confident), both losses are small. As the prediction drops toward 0 for a true 1 — a confident wrong answer — squared error stays below 1, but logistic loss explodes. That asymmetry is what forces logistic regression to avoid overconfidence on hard cases.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
gradient descent: hiking downhill
\[\beta \leftarrow \beta - \eta \cdot \nabla L(\beta)\]


\(\nabla L(\beta)\) = gradient (uphill direction); step the opposite way
\(\eta\) = learning rate (step size)



logistic loss is convex — one valley, one global minimum


(as long as the data isn’t perfectly separable — then the minimum runs off to infinity)

Picture the loss as a landscape over the β coefficients. Gradient descent is literal hiking. At each step, compute the gradient — the vector of partial derivatives, which points uphill — and step the opposite direction. η is the step size. Too small and it’s slow; too big and you overshoot. The magic property of logistic regression is that the landscape is convex: a single bowl with no false valleys. So long as the data isn’t pathological (like perfectly separable, which sends the minimum to infinity), gradient descent finds the global minimum regardless of starting point.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
watch it converge
Q: will the loss curve drop smoothly, oscillate, or bounce around?








50 iterations on age + BMI logistic regression — loss drops fast, then settles

We hand-rolled gradient descent on a 2-feature logistic regression so we can watch it work. The loss drops sharply in the first handful of iterations, then settles. Because the problem is convex, “settles” means we’ve found the minimum. sklearn’s LogisticRegression uses a fancier variant (L-BFGS or similar), but the intuition is the same: start somewhere, step downhill, stop when the gradient is small.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text: accuracy lies under class imbalance

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
fit it, test it
model = LogisticRegression().fit(X_train_scaled, y_train)
test_acc = model.score(X_test_scaled, y_test)
print(f"Test accuracy: {test_acc:.3f}")

Test accuracy: 0.855


85.5% accurate — sounds great, right?

Fit our 9-feature logistic regression on the Framingham training set — the full feature set, with standardized features. Compute accuracy on the held-out test set. 85.5%. A B+ on a typical grading curve. Hold this number in mind. Now: what’s the accuracy of the dumbest possible model?

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
but wait
the “always predict no CHD” baseline:
Q: what accuracy does it get?

baseline accuracy  = 0.848
our model accuracy = 0.855
improvement        = 0.007


our fancy classifier barely beats the null

Predict “no CHD” for every single patient — no features, no fitting, nothing. Its accuracy is 1 - base rate = 85% because 85% of patients actually don’t develop CHD. Our model’s 85.5% is only slightly better. The 85.5% accuracy number was almost entirely driven by class imbalance, not by the model learning anything useful. This is the accuracy trap — and it shows up whenever one class dominates. We need different metrics.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
the accuracy trap



Warning


accuracy measures how often you’re right overall
when one class dominates, being right about that class is easy and uninformative




Before moving on, make the general claim. Accuracy is a population-averaged metric, weighted by how often each class appears. In an imbalanced problem (medical screening, fraud, equipment failure, spam), the common class dominates the average. Metrics that weight the two classes differently — or that look at each class separately — tell a more honest story.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
predict-then-verify: the confusion matrix
1,096 test patients · 15% CHD base rate · threshold 0.5




predicted no CHD
predicted CHD




actually no CHD
?
?


actually CHD
?
?




the model is very conservative — which cell dominates?

Before showing the filled matrix, have students predict. 1,096 test patients, ~85% are true negatives by base rate, so the TN cell (top-left) will dominate. But that says nothing about whether the model is useful. The more revealing question is the bottom row — of the ~167 CHD patients, how many does the model catch? Force them to guess before revealing.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
the confusion matrix


of 167 actual CHD cases, we caught 17

Reveal. True negatives dominate (922) — exactly as predicted. But the bottom row is the story: 17 caught, 150 missed. The model is correctly saying “not CHD” to most people and missing the ones that matter. Accuracy hid this because the 922 true negatives drown out everything else.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
precision vs. recall


precision \[\frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}}\]
“I flagged this patient — should I trust the flag?”

recall (sensitivity) \[\frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}}\]
“of all the sick people, how many did we find?”


two metrics → two different stories

Precision is the precision of your positive predictions — when you say yes, how often are you right? Recall is the completeness of your positive detection — of all the true positives out there, how many did you catch? They move in opposite directions when you change the threshold. Require higher confidence before flagging → precision up, recall down. Lower the bar → more flags, precision drops, recall climbs.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
our model’s precision and recall
Precision: 0.65
  → Of patients flagged as CHD, 65% actually had it

Recall:    0.10
  → Of actual CHD patients (167 in test set), we caught 17

accuracy hid a disaster — the model catches almost nobody

Precision is decent — when the model does flag someone, about two-thirds of the time it’s right. But recall is abysmal. Of all 167 actual CHD patients in the test set, we caught 17. For a screening tool this is a complete failure. The 85% accuracy masked that the model is barely functioning on the task we actually care about.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
which type of error would you rather the model make?

CHD screening: FP → $500 follow-up · FN → $50K emergency care
spam filter: FP → real email lost → missed meeting · FN → spam in inbox → mild annoyance

for each: flag more, or flag fewer?



30 seconds: think
1 minute: neighbor



Two separate scenarios, same question. In medicine, missing a disease case costs far more than flagging an extra patient for follow-up — so we’d want a low threshold, flag liberally, keep recall high. In spam filtering, the costs flip: a false positive (a real email sent to spam) is worse than letting a piece of spam through — so we’d use a high threshold, flag conservatively, keep precision high. The point: the right threshold depends on the business problem, not the statistics.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text: threshold choice is a cost-benefit call

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
precision-recall across thresholds


each annotated point is one threshold — as recall climbs, precision falls


no point dominates — you must choose

Rather than pick a single threshold, sweep all of them. For each threshold, compute precision and recall and plot the point. Perfect model: a point at (1, 1). Our curve shows the operational tradeoff directly. At t = 0.5 (top-left), precision is decent but recall is terrible. Slide down-and-right and you catch more real cases but each flag is less trustworthy. There is no dominant point — you pick one based on costs, which we just discussed.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
another view: ROC


AUC ≈ 0.75 — concordance: pick one CHD and one non-CHD patient; the model ranks the CHD patient higher 75% of the time (ties count as half)


sounds great, right?

Same threshold sweep, different axes: plot true positive rate (= recall) against false positive rate (= FP / all negatives). Perfect model: hugs the top-left corner. Random guessing: the diagonal. AUC = area under. Our 0.75 has a clean interpretation — concordance probability. Pick one CHD patient and one non-CHD patient; the model ranks the CHD patient higher 75% of the time. That’s threshold-free ranking quality. Deliver “sounds great, right?” with the same setup as the accuracy gotcha — the subgroup and calibration sections will break that confidence.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
section: PR vs ROC — when to use which
text:
PR vs ROC — when to use which


PR curve
axes: precision · recall
depends on base rate
operational view — most honest when the positive class is rare

ROC curve
axes: TPR · FPR
invariant to base rate
threshold-free summary (AUC) · compare models across datasets


same tradeoff, different invariances

Two cuts of the same threshold sweep. The PR curve speaks to the operational question — “when I flag a patient, am I right?” — which is exactly what matters in imbalanced screening. It depends on the base rate, so it’s not directly comparable across populations. The ROC curve and its AUC are invariant to how many negatives you have: shuffle in ten times more healthy patients and the ROC doesn’t change, while PR gets worse. So for a fixed problem with a rare positive class — CHD, fraud, readmissions — the PR curve is usually the more honest view. For cross-dataset comparison or a ranking-quality summary, use ROC + AUC. In this class we’ll typically report both.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
threshold effect on metrics


low threshold → catches more, but each flag is less reliable

Concrete numbers. At the default 0.5 threshold, accuracy is 86%, precision 65%, recall 10% — we flag only 26 of 1,096 test patients. Drop the threshold to 0.1 and we flag 624 patients (57% of the test set). Recall jumps to 85% — we catch almost all CHD cases — but precision drops to 23% (most flags are wrong) and accuracy falls to 54%, below baseline. What to pick? Depends on follow-up test cost, ICU bed availability, patient outcomes. No purely statistical answer.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text: aggregate metrics hide local failures

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
section: base rate neglect: commit to an answer
text:
base rate neglect: commit to an answer
a vendor pitches a cancer screening test as 99% accurate:

99% sensitivity — catches 99% of cancer cases (TP rate)
99% specificity — correctly clears 99% of healthy patients (TN rate)

cancer prevalence: 0.5%
a patient tests positive — P(cancer | positive)?


A. 99% · B. 67% · C. 33% · D. 1%


Force the commit before the walkthrough. Most people — including most doctors in published studies — pick A. The room should poll. Hands up for A / B / C / D. Let them stay committed for a beat. Then pivot to the 10,000-patient walkthrough on the next slide: the answer is C.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
work it out on 10,000 patients


false positives swamp true positives when the condition is rare

Out of 10,000 patients: 50 actually have cancer (0.5% prevalence). Of those, the test catches 99%, so ~50 true positives. The other 9,950 are healthy. The test misclassifies 1% of them, so ~100 false positives. Total positive results: ~150. Of those 150, only ~50 are real — about 33%. The vendor’s “99% accuracy” number was true; the patient’s posterior probability was just 33%. That’s base rate neglect, and it’s why headline accuracy numbers on rare conditions are mostly meaningless.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
aggregate AUC on our Framingham model is 0.75
is that good?
for which patients?



30 seconds: think
pair: which patient group do you expect the model to fail on, and why?



Setup for the subgroup reveal. Overall AUC of 0.75 sounds OK. But AUC is a population-averaged metric. A model can ace the majority and fail the minority. The question is: for which patients do we expect this model to struggle? Age is obvious given what we’ve seen — but let students think before revealing.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
subgroup AUC by age


for patients under 40, AUC = 0.36 — below 0.5; inverting the ranking would do better

Compute AUC separately in each age group. For patients over 40, the model is decent — AUC above 0.6 across groups. For under-40 patients, AUC drops to 0.36 — below 0.5, worse than random guessing. Inverting the model’s ranking would actually do better in this subgroup. The positive class there is tiny — only ~6 CHD cases in 191 patients — so the estimate is noisy, but the message is unambiguous: the model has no useful signal for young patients. It learned “older people get heart disease” and nothing else.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
predicted probabilities by age group


for older patients, red (CHD) sits above blue — for young patients, the two distributions overlap

Same failure in a different view. For patients 50+, the red (CHD) distribution sits noticeably above the blue (no CHD) — the model assigns higher probabilities to the sick patients on average. For patients under 40, both distributions are bunched low and overlap heavily — the model barely separates them. In practice this is dangerous: young patients who do develop CHD are exactly the ones where early intervention pays off, and the signal our model uses doesn’t help here.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
section: calibration — do probabilities mean what they say?
text:
calibration — do probabilities mean what they say?


bin predictions into deciles → does “10% risk” mean 10% observed rate?

The last aggregate lie to unmask. Logistic regression outputs probabilities, not just classifications. But do those probabilities mean what they claim? If the model says “20% risk” for a group, do 20% of that group actually develop CHD? To check: bin predictions into deciles (10 equal-count buckets), compute the observed CHD rate in each bin, plot observed vs predicted. A perfectly calibrated model sits on the diagonal. Our model tracks the diagonal reasonably well across the range where most patients live. It’s well-calibrated — even though we just saw it fails badly on other dimensions. Calibration is orthogonal to ranking ability.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
calibration ≠ AUC


AUC measures ranking
does the model rank CHD patients above non-CHD?

calibration measures absolute probabilities
is “10% risk” really a 10% rate?


a model can have good AUC and bad calibration (or vice versa)


which matters depends on how you use the output

These two metrics measure different properties. AUC is threshold-free ranking — only the order of predictions matters; rescale them any way and AUC is unchanged. Calibration depends on the actual values — a model that outputs “0.20” for patients who are truly 0.40 risk has perfect ranking but awful calibration. If you use the probabilities directly for cost-benefit tradeoffs (Bayesian decision theory), calibration matters. If you just pick the top-k patients to screen, only ranking matters.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text: closing the loop

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
back to the readmissions hook
the question that opened the lecture
a hospital’s model is “85% accurate” — deploy it?

now you know what to ask:

what’s the baseline? (accuracy trap)
what’s precision and recall at the chosen threshold?
ROC curve — is the chosen threshold a good point on the tradeoff?
does the model work across all patient groups?
are the probabilities calibrated?


Close the loop. We opened with a hospital’s 85% accurate readmissions model. Now you have the toolkit to diagnose it. First, check the baseline — is “predict none” 85%? If so, accuracy alone tells you nothing. Next, look at precision and recall at whatever threshold is being used. Then the full ROC curve and AUC to see performance across thresholds. Then subgroup breakdowns — is the model equally good across age, race, insurance type? Finally, calibration — if the model says “40% readmission risk,” is it really 40%? The hospital’s decision on where to operate on the precision-recall curve is a cost-benefit call about intervention dollars, staff availability, and patient outcomes — not a statistics question.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
key takeaways

accuracy is misleading with imbalanced classes — check the baseline first
the loss function determines what the model finds: logistic loss penalizes confident wrong answers
gradient descent on a convex loss finds the global minimum (unique when the data isn’t separable)
precision / recall reveal what accuracy hides — the right threshold is a cost-benefit call
AUC summarizes ranking across thresholds; calibration checks absolute probabilities
always check subgroup performance — aggregate metrics hide local failures


Six takeaways. The first three are about building a classifier: loss design, optimization, fitting. The last three are about evaluating one. The thread through all six: don’t trust a single number. Every aggregate can lie, and the way you dig into the lie depends on how your downstream decision uses the model.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
next time

Chapter 8: bootstrap — quantify uncertainty in any estimate (accuracy, AUC, odds ratio, …)
Chapter 12: formal inference on logistic coefficients (confidence intervals, p-values)
Chapter 13: decision trees — same classification problem, very different model


Today closed Act 1. Act 2 opens with the bootstrap — a resampling tool that lets us put confidence intervals on any statistic, including today’s classification metrics. Then Chapter 12 returns to logistic regression to do formal inference on coefficients. And in Act 3, trees and forests extend classification to nonlinear decision boundaries.

title: Lecture 3: Data Munging — Facilitation Script
text:
Total time: 80 minutes (~75 planned + 5 buffer) Notebook: Colab link Chapter: Book chapter 3 (lec03-munging.qmd)




Open the Colab notebook and clear all outputs
Test that the notebook runs (scorecard.csv loads, field_of_study.csv loads)
Have the slide deck open in presenter view
Check that the feedback QR code link works
Bookmark the Stanford earnings section of the notebook — you’ll want to show it live







Slide: “concept check from last time”
Display the Airbnb question. Give 15 seconds of silent thought. Advance to reveal the question: “what information would you need?”
Students should say: comparable listings, location, neighborhood, season, amenities. Push back if anyone says “just the average”: “the average of what? All SF listings? All 4-bedrooms? All 4-bedrooms within a mile?” This sets up today’s theme: every analytical choice is a decision.



Slide: montage — debt-vs-GDP chart
“2010. Two Harvard economists publish a paper that becomes the foundation for austerity budgets across Europe. Government debt above 90% of GDP means negative growth — that’s the headline. Three years later, a grad student trying to replicate their results for a homework assignment discovers the error.”



Slide: “the spreadsheet”
“Here’s the actual spreadsheet. The formula at the bottom says AVERAGE L30 to L44. But the data goes to row 49. Five countries — Australia, Austria, Belgium, Canada, Denmark — are excluded. The corrected result flips the finding from minus 0.1% to plus 2.2%.”
Let students absorb. Don’t rush this.



Slide: “three data disasters”
Three columns. Read each briefly: “A cell range. A row limit. An auto-conversion. Three different tools, one pattern: a tool made a decision without warning.”



Slide: “every cleaning decision changes the answer”
Pause 3 seconds. Don’t speak.



Slide: “today’s concepts”
Read the five items quickly. “By the end of class, you’ll have a checklist for every new dataset.”



Slide: “recap: what we found last time”
Brief. “Last time: Airbnb data was relatively tidy. NaN for missing values, pandas handled it. But we noticed some dtypes looked wrong. Today: real datasets are rarely so cooperative.”







Slide: “block 1: the many faces of missing data”



Slide: “today’s dataset: College Scorecard”
“The US Department of Education publishes this every year. 7,700 institutions, 122 columns. It has something most datasets don’t: a data dictionary, methodology documentation, public updates. Before you clean a dataset, ask where it came from.”
Advance to reveal the Kaggle contrast. “Contrast: a random CSV on Kaggle with no documentation. You can’t even know if the data was collected or generated by an AI.”



Slide: “data provenance”
Show definition box. Then advance through the checkmarks. “In the AI era, provenance is breaking down — synthetic datasets, undocumented web scrapes, training data contamination.”



Slides: “a column that should be numeric” → “the culprit: PrivacySuppressed”
First slide: “MD_EARN_WNE_P10. Median earnings 10 years after enrollment. Should be a number. dtype: string.” Pause. “Why?”
Advance to reveal. “PrivacySuppressed. A human-readable phrase sitting in a numeric column. 480 schools. One string forces the entire column to be non-numeric.”



Slide: “the missingness zoo”
Walk through the table quickly. “NaN is the easy case — pandas handles it. Everything else requires active detection.” Don’t dwell on each row.



Slide: Demo slide
Switch to the notebook. Show: 1. Load scorecard, check dtypes, note the str columns 2. Find non-numeric values in MD_EARN_WNE_P10 3. Count PrivacySuppressed across all columns (7 columns affected) 4. Bin schools by enrollment size, show suppression rate by size
Don’t run the entire chapter — just enough to establish the pattern.



Slide: discussion — “if you drop those rows…”
“Think for one minute: if you drop the PrivacySuppressed rows, what kind of schools remain? Would the average earnings go up or down?”
After 1 min think + 2 min pair: take 2-3 responses.
If stuck: “Think about which schools have small cohorts — rural? specialized? under-resourced?”
Key insight: Dropping these rows removes small, rural, specialized schools. The remaining sample is biased toward large institutions. Average earnings probably goes up — small schools tend to serve lower-earning populations. This is selection bias.



Slide: bar chart — suppression by size
“Nearly a third of the smallest schools are suppressed. Among 2,000+ students: about 1%. This isn’t random.”



Slide: “three patterns of missingness”
Show definition box. “MCAR: truly random — mold destroys random records. MAR: depends on things we can see — older patients skip surveys, and we have their age. MNAR: depends on the missing value itself — the sickest patients don’t report, and we never observe their outcomes.”
Advance to reveal: “College Scorecard earnings: MNAR. Small cohort means suppressed. Small schools are systematically different.”
Vivid example from the book: “In ambulance data, a missing heart rate often means the EMTs were too busy saving the patient. The missing value is the signal.”







Slide: “block 2: joining datasets”



Slide: “why join?”
“The Scorecard comes in two files. Institution-level: one row per school. Program-level: one row per school-program pair.” Advance to reveal the questions. “To answer richer questions, we need to combine them.”



Slides: toy example → inner join → left join (3 slides)
Show the Students and Majors tables side by side. “Before I show you the result — which students survive an inner join on major_id?” Take quick answers.
Advance to inner join result. “Alice and Bob survive. Carol is gone — no match for 103. History is gone — no match for 104. Neither table warns you.”
Advance to left join. “All three students survive. Carol’s major becomes NaN — a signal that she has no matching major. That signal is lost in an inner join.”



Slide: “one-to-many: row multiplication”
Show left and right tables. “Two rows on the left, three on the right, all with key A.” Pause. “How many rows in the result?”
Advance to reveal. “Six. 2 × 3 = 6. Joins can GROW your data, not just shrink it.”



Slide: “defense: check your key before joining”
Show the assert. “One line of code. An assert that passes is documentation. An assert that fails is an alarm.”



Slide: discussion — “predict: how many rows…”
“The scorecard has 7,703 institutions. Programs has 228,000 rows across 5,500 institutions. How many rows will the inner join produce? More or fewer than 7,703?”
Most students will say “fewer” — they’re thinking of inner joins as filters. The answer is ~216,000 — a massive expansion because each institution matches to many programs.
If stuck: “Each institution matches to many programs. Stanford alone has 180+.”
Key insight: The inner join is one-to-many — it expands, not contracts. Students who predicted “fewer” were thinking about key overlap (correct for one-to-one joins) but missed the one-to-many expansion.



Slide: Demo slide
Switch to notebook. Show: 1. The assert on UNITID uniqueness 2. The inner join: 7,703 → ~216,000 rows 3. The left join: preserves all 7,703 institutions 4. The bar chart comparing row counts
“7,700 rows became 216,000. And neither join warned you.”



Slide: “after every join: check row counts”
Walk through the table. “Inner joins can gain AND lose rows at the same time — one-to-many expansion plus unmatched key dropping. Left joins gain rows but don’t lose them.”
Advance to reveal: “The join type is a decision, not a default.”







Slide: “block 3: when tools betray you”



Slide: “three approaches to missing earnings”
Show the table. “Same question, three approaches. Drop rows, fill with mean, ignore NaN. They all give the same mean.”
Advance to reveal: “If they all give the same mean, why does it matter?”
“Approach 1 removes entire rows — those schools vanish from every column. Approach 2 shrinks the standard deviation — every imputed value sits at the center. Approach 3 lets pandas skip NaN column by column.”



Slide: bar chart — missing by ownership
“36% of for-profit schools have missing earnings. 9% of public schools. Dropping rows changes WHICH schools remain.”
Advance: “This is informative missingness — the absence of data tells you something about the institution.”



Slide: discussion — “an AI calls .dropna()…”
“An AI ‘cleans’ the Scorecard by calling dropna and reports average earnings of $45,000. Name two questions you’d ask, focused on what was lost in the cleaning step.”
If stuck: “How many rows survived? Are the dropped rows systematically different? What was the missingness rate?”
Key insight: The questions to ask: (1) How many rows survived — what fraction of the data was lost? (2) Are the dropped schools systematically different — were small/for-profit schools disproportionately dropped? (3) Which approach was used and why?



Slide: “Stanford: a case study in hidden complexity”
“Median earnings: $86,000. SAT average: 1465. Sounds good?”
Advance to reveal: “$86K qualifies as ‘Low Income’ in Santa Clara County. The threshold for one person is $111,700.”
Advance to the three factors: “Title IV only — many Stanford students don’t take federal aid. 10 years after enrollment — includes years in grad school. All fields pooled — CS at $139K, English at $31K.”
“The gap between the Scorecard number and your expectations is itself a data-cleaning lesson.”



Slide: “type coercion: tools that corrupt data”
“pd.to_numeric with errors=‘coerce’ converts PrivacySuppressed to NaN. No warning, no count.” Show the defense code. “Always check what you lost.”



Slide: “sentinel values”
Quick. “-999 looks like a number. Mean WITH sentinels: -208°F. Mean WITHOUT: 72.9°F. Unlike NaN, sentinel values participate in every computation.”



Slide: “the identifier trap”
Quick. “OPEID with leading zeros — stored as integer, zeros vanish. Later join against zero-padded strings: zero matches, no error. ZIP codes, phone numbers, IDs: codes, not numbers.”



Slide: discussion — “you upload the Scorecard to ChatGPT…”
“ChatGPT ranks schools by raw MD_EARN_WNE_P10 after dropping all missing data. Stanford is #47. Small groups: identify at least two problems.”
If stuck: “How many rows survived the dropna? What does MD_EARN_WNE_P10 actually measure — and who’s in the sample?”
Key insight: (1) Raw earnings confounded by selectivity — high-SAT schools admit students who’d earn well anywhere. (2) Dropping rows loses most small/for-profit schools. (3) The metric pools all fields and measures 10yr after enrollment. (4) May not be reproducible across sessions.



Slide: “why we write code”
Quick. Two columns: spreadsheets hide logic, code makes decisions visible. “Good code is deterministic.”



Slide: comparison table
Walk through three rows. “Use AI to write code, not to replace understanding.”







Slide: “data munging checklist”
Walk through all 8 items briefly. “This is the checklist you run every time you encounter a new dataset. Each item connects to a concrete example from today.”



Slide: blockquote
Read aloud. Let it sit for 3 seconds.



Slide: “synthesis”
Map each bullet back to the lecture: “Missing data — block 1. Joins — block 2. Every cleaning choice — the whole lecture. Code makes decisions visible — the Reinhart-Rogoff opening. Domain knowledge — the Stanford case study.”



Slide: “next time: linear algebra”
“We can clean and join data. Now we need a language for modeling it. Vectors, linear combinations, column space.”



Slide: “before next class”
“Read Chapter 3. Try the notebook. Quiz 1 is Wednesday — covers Lectures 1 through 3.”



Slide: feedback QR code
“One minute. What was most useful? What was most confusing? Scan the QR code.”






If running long: Skip the identifier trap slide (36 sec) and abbreviate the AI comparison table (just say the punchline).
If running short: Expand the Stanford case study with live notebook exploration of program-level earnings. Show the PrivacySuppressed entries for MS&E. Let students browse their own majors.
Block 3 is the densest section. If time is tight, the checklist slide and AI-vs-code slide can be abbreviated since they recap ideas already demonstrated.

title: Lecture 4: Linear Models — Facilitation Script
text:
Duration: 80 minutes Primary dataset: NYC Airbnb (5-listing toy example + 28,761 full dataset) Structure: Opening (5 min) → Block 1: Vectors (15 min + 5 min) → Block 2: Linear models + regression (15 min + 5 min) → Block 3: Multiple regression + R² (15 min + 5 min) → Closing (5 min) → Buffer (5 min)



Hook (slide 2, dark): “How much is a bathroom worth?” — an Airbnb host adding a second bathroom. We’ll answer this by the end of the lecture.
Agenda (slide 3): vectors, linear combinations, column space, regression, R². Jump straight into Block 1.






Data points are vectors. Once we see them as vectors, we can measure distance (norm), similarity (inner product), and direction (cosine similarity). This language will let us understand what a linear model does.



Slide 9 (two views): Show the toy table. Two views: row = point, column = vector. Emphasize this is the same data seen two ways.
Slide 10 (scatter): Point at “Listings 1 & 5” — same features, same point. Listing 3 is farthest away.
Slide 11 (difference vector): Code on slide — no context switch needed. The difference vector says how they differ.
Slide 12 (norm): Definition box. “Generalizes the Pythagorean theorem.” Show the code: np.linalg.norm(diff) = 2.24.
Slide 13 (inner product): Definition box. Positive = same direction, negative = opposite, zero = perpendicular. Show code.
Slide 14 (cosine similarity): Formula, then code. “0.98 means very similar proportions.”




Format: Poll + predict-then-reveal (3 min)
Setup: “beds vs bedrooms — high or low cosine? beds vs reviews — high or low?”
If stuck: “If you know bedrooms, can you guess beds? What about reviews?”
Key insight: beds/bedrooms = 0.65 (redundant), beds/reviews = 0.10 (independent). Independent features are more valuable.
Reveal: Show slide 16 with the answer table.








A linear model is a weighted sum of feature columns. The set of all possible predictions = column space. Regression finds the closest point in that space to the true prices.
Slide 17 (dark): “what can your model reach?”
Slide 18 (linear combination code): Show the computation — 100×bedrooms + 50×bathrooms. Code + output on the slide. “This is ALL a linear model does.”
Slide 19 (definition): Linear combination definition.
Slide 20 (span image): The lattice of all integer-weight combinations. “With real weights, you can reach every point in the plane.”




Format: Think-pair-share (3 min)
Setup: “Can you reach every point in R²? What if v₁ and v₂ point the same direction?”
If stuck: “What shape do you get from multiples of a single vector?”
Key insight: Non-parallel = span all of R². Parallel = only a line. Redundant features don’t help.

Slide 22 (3D projection): Crucial image. “Two vectors in R³ span a plane. The target is off the plane — the closest point is the projection.” Point out the right-angle mark.
Slides 23-24 (column space): Definition, then code. X @ beta computes everything at once.
Slide 25 (dark): “regression = projection”
Slides 26-28 (real data): Load 28,761 listings. Show scatter + regression line. ŷ = 71 + 49 × bedrooms.
Slide 29 (inline Q/A): “What does the intercept mean?” Reveal: $71 for a studio.
Slide 30 (why squared?): Brief — two reasons (projection geometry, conditional mean). “We could choose differently — and we will, in Ch 13.”







Adding features expands the column space. Multiple regression lets us “hold constant.” The normal equations explain why. R² measures fit quality. Residual plots reveal what the model misses.
Slides 31-32 (regression to the mean): Galton story. “Since |r| < 1, predictions are pulled toward the mean.” Connect to the name “regression.”




Format: Predict-then-reveal (3 min)
Setup: “Player scores 45 (average: 25). Predict next game?”
If stuck: “Is the 45 more likely a fluke or the new normal?”
Key insight: Below 45, above 25. Coaches who overreact to extreme games are ignoring regression to the mean.

Slides 34-35 (multiple regression): Code + coefficient table. “Each bathroom ~$22, private room −$90.”
Slide 36 (answer!): “~$22/night for that second bathroom.” R² jumped from 0.16 to 0.45.




Format: Poll (2 min)
Setup: “Positive, negative, or zero?”
Key insight: Negative. Cheaper listings get booked more → more reviews. Association ≠ causation.

Slides 38-39 (orthogonality): Column space cartoon + normal equations. “The residual has zero dot product with every feature.”
Slide 40 (verify in code): Show the orthogonality check — both dot products ≈ 0.
Slide 41 (R² formula): Pythagorean decomposition. Total = explained + residual.




Format: Think-pair-share (4 min)
Setup: “Think about the geometry: adding a column makes the column space…”

title: Lecture 4: Linear Models — Facilitation Script
text:
Hook (slide 2, dark): “How much is a bathroom worth?” — an Airbnb host adding a second bathroom. We’ll answer this by the end of the lecture.
Agenda (slide 3): vectors, linear combinations, column space, regression, R². Jump straight into Block 1.

title: Lecture 4: Linear Models — Facilitation Script
text:
Data points are vectors. Once we see them as vectors, we can measure distance (norm), similarity (inner product), and direction (cosine similarity). This language will let us understand what a linear model does.



Slide 9 (two views): Show the toy table. Two views: row = point, column = vector. Emphasize this is the same data seen two ways.
Slide 10 (scatter): Point at “Listings 1 & 5” — same features, same point. Listing 3 is farthest away.
Slide 11 (difference vector): Code on slide — no context switch needed. The difference vector says how they differ.
Slide 12 (norm): Definition box. “Generalizes the Pythagorean theorem.” Show the code: np.linalg.norm(diff) = 2.24.
Slide 13 (inner product): Definition box. Positive = same direction, negative = opposite, zero = perpendicular. Show code.
Slide 14 (cosine similarity): Formula, then code. “0.98 means very similar proportions.”




Format: Poll + predict-then-reveal (3 min)
Setup: “beds vs bedrooms — high or low cosine? beds vs reviews — high or low?”
If stuck: “If you know bedrooms, can you guess beds? What about reviews?”
Key insight: beds/bedrooms = 0.65 (redundant), beds/reviews = 0.10 (independent). Independent features are more valuable.
Reveal: Show slide 16 with the answer table.

title: Lecture 4: Linear Models — Facilitation Script
text:
A linear model is a weighted sum of feature columns. The set of all possible predictions = column space. Regression finds the closest point in that space to the true prices.
Slide 17 (dark): “what can your model reach?”
Slide 18 (linear combination code): Show the computation — 100×bedrooms + 50×bathrooms. Code + output on the slide. “This is ALL a linear model does.”
Slide 19 (definition): Linear combination definition.
Slide 20 (span image): The lattice of all integer-weight combinations. “With real weights, you can reach every point in the plane.”




Format: Think-pair-share (3 min)
Setup: “Can you reach every point in R²? What if v₁ and v₂ point the same direction?”
If stuck: “What shape do you get from multiples of a single vector?”
Key insight: Non-parallel = span all of R². Parallel = only a line. Redundant features don’t help.

Slide 22 (3D projection): Crucial image. “Two vectors in R³ span a plane. The target is off the plane — the closest point is the projection.” Point out the right-angle mark.
Slides 23-24 (column space): Definition, then code. X @ beta computes everything at once.
Slide 25 (dark): “regression = projection”
Slides 26-28 (real data): Load 28,761 listings. Show scatter + regression line. ŷ = 71 + 49 × bedrooms.
Slide 29 (inline Q/A): “What does the intercept mean?” Reveal: $71 for a studio.
Slide 30 (why squared?): Brief — two reasons (projection geometry, conditional mean). “We could choose differently — and we will, in Ch 13.”

title: Lecture 4: Linear Models — Facilitation Script
text:
Adding features expands the column space. Multiple regression lets us “hold constant.” The normal equations explain why. R² measures fit quality. Residual plots reveal what the model misses.
Slides 31-32 (regression to the mean): Galton story. “Since |r| < 1, predictions are pulled toward the mean.” Connect to the name “regression.”




Format: Predict-then-reveal (3 min)
Setup: “Player scores 45 (average: 25). Predict next game?”
If stuck: “Is the 45 more likely a fluke or the new normal?”
Key insight: Below 45, above 25. Coaches who overreact to extreme games are ignoring regression to the mean.

Slides 34-35 (multiple regression): Code + coefficient table. “Each bathroom ~$22, private room −$90.”
Slide 36 (answer!): “~$22/night for that second bathroom.” R² jumped from 0.16 to 0.45.




Format: Poll (2 min)
Setup: “Positive, negative, or zero?”
Key insight: Negative. Cheaper listings get booked more → more reviews. Association ≠ causation.

Slides 38-39 (orthogonality): Column space cartoon + normal equations. “The residual has zero dot product with every feature.”
Slide 40 (verify in code): Show the orthogonality check — both dot products ≈ 0.
Slide 41 (R² formula): Pythagorean decomposition. Total = explained + residual.




Format: Think-pair-share (4 min)
Setup: “Think about the geometry: adding a column makes the column space…”
If stuck: “Can a projection onto a bigger space be farther from the target?”
Key insight: Training R² always increases. Test R² can decrease (overfitting, Ch 7).

Slide 42 (residual plots): Fan shape = heteroscedasticity. “The model misses something.”

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Facilitation Script
text:
Duration: 75 minutes Primary dataset: Framingham Heart Study (CHD, 15% base rate) Hook: 85% accuracy model that catches zero patients Structure: Opening (5 min) → Block 1: Mechanics (~20 min) → Block 2: Accuracy trap (~15 min + 5 min poll) → Block 3: Thresholds (~10 min + 5 min poll) → Block 4: Subgroups + calibration (~10 min + 5 min poll) → Closing (2 min) → Buffer



Slides 1–2 (dark hook + readmissions setup). Open with the drama: the model was 85% accurate and caught zero. Pause. Then the readmissions setup — base rate is 15%, null-model baseline looks like a good model. Key: this is not a toy problem. CMS penalties are real; hospitals actually deploy models like this.
Slide 3 (agenda): Four blocks. Mechanics → accuracy trap → thresholds → what aggregates hide.






We need a classifier. Naive OLS fails because predictions leave [0, 1]. Sigmoid trick → logistic regression. Fit with gradient descent on a convex loss.
Slide 4 (binary outcome): Introduce Framingham. 4,240 patients, 10-year followup. Outcome is 0/1. Stake the vocabulary: CHD = 1 = positive class; no CHD = 0 = negative class. Positive = detected, not desirable — the positive CHD test is bad news. This matters when we talk sensitivity/specificity later.
Slide 5 (linreg fails): Show the scatter. Predictions below 0 and above 1. “You can’t use OLS because nothing constrains predictions to [0, 1].”
Slide 6 (sigmoid): “Rather than constrain OLS, transform what we model.” Push z through σ. Show the S-shape. z = 0 maps to p = 0.5.
Slide 7 (log-odds table): This is the same idea from a different angle. Walk the rows. Log-odds range from −∞ to +∞ — exactly what a linear model can output. Logistic regression = linear model of log-odds.
Slide 8 (Framingham age fit): “We see the lower portion of the S because the base rate is low.” Age alone doesn’t get anyone above 30% predicted risk.
Slide 9 (logistic loss formula): No closed form. Pick a loss. Emphasize the confident wrong penalty.
Slide 10 (loss comparison): Visual — squared stays bounded, logistic explodes. This is the asymmetry that forces the model to avoid overconfidence on hard cases.
Slide 11 (gradient descent): Hiking metaphor. ∇L = uphill, step the opposite way. η = step size. Convex = single bowl (assuming data isn’t separable — don’t belabor, just note).
Slide 12 (GD convergence): Loss drops fast, settles. This is 50 iterations on a 2-feature model; sklearn uses a fancier variant (L-BFGS).







Fit the model, compute accuracy, celebrate → reveal the baseline is identical → unmask accuracy as misleading under class imbalance → introduce confusion matrix, precision, recall.
Slide 13 (dark): Block break.
Slide 14 (fit + test accuracy): 85.5%. Hold it in mind.
Slide 15 (baseline reveal): “Always predict no CHD” scores 84.8%. Our model improves by 0.7 points. This is the lecture’s most important beat — land it slowly.
Slide 16 (accuracy trap callout): General principle. Accuracy is population-averaged. With imbalance, the common class dominates.
Slide 17 (confusion matrix): Walk through the four cells. TN dominates. Bottom row is where we care about — only 17 of 167 CHD cases caught. “Accuracy hid this because there are so many true negatives.”
Slide 18 (precision vs recall definitions): Two metrics, two questions. Precision = “trust the flag?” Recall = “of all the sick, how many did we find?”
Slide 19 (our P and R): 63% precision, 8% recall. A disaster for a screening tool.
Slide 20 (discussion poll): FP vs FN costs — $500 vs tens of thousands. Students commit. Then flip: spam filter reverses the costs. Key lesson: the threshold is a cost-benefit call, not a statistical one. 2 minutes max. Don’t resolve — transition to Block 3.







Sweep thresholds. ROC for ranking; PR for tradeoff; actual metrics at 5 thresholds to make it concrete.
Slide 21 (dark): Block break.
Slide 22 (ROC): Perfect model hugs top-left. Ours is 0.75 AUC. Concordance probability — “pick one CHD, one non-CHD, model ranks correctly 75% of the time.” Clean, threshold-free.
Slide 23 (PR curve): Same information, different view. No dominant point — you must choose.
Slide 24 (threshold effect bars): Concrete numbers at 5 thresholds. Low threshold → catch more, trust each flag less.







Three ways a “good” aggregate metric can mask disaster: base rates, subgroups, calibration.
Slide 25 (dark): Block break.
Slide 26 (base rate callout): Set up the 99% claim. Pause. Ask students to commit to an answer before the reveal.
Slide 27 (10,000 patient visualization): Walk through the arithmetic. 50 cancer cases + 100 false positives = ~33% posterior. “The ‘99% accuracy’ was true; the patient’s P(cancer | positive) is 33%.” Base rate neglect. This is why mass screening programs are controversial.
Slide 28 (subgroup discussion poll): Let students predict which group the model fails for. Most will say “young patients” because age is the dominant feature. Good — set up the reveal. 2 minutes max.
Slide 29 (subgroup AUC): Reveal. Under-40 AUC ≈ 0.36. Below 0.5 = worse than random. Inverting the ranking would do better. Caveat: only ~6 CHD cases in 191 patients, so the estimate is noisy — but the direction is real. The model learned “older = sicker” and nothing else.
Slide 30 (boxplot): Same failure in a different view. Young: everything bunched at zero. Old: red above blue. The model is essentially blind to young-patient risk.
Slide 31 (calibration plot): New question. Do probabilities mean what they say? Decile bins → observed vs predicted. Our model tracks the diagonal reasonably well. It’s well-calibrated, despite the ranking failures above. These are different properties.
Slide 32 (AUC vs calibration): Two properties. AUC = ranking, invariant to monotone rescaling. Calibration = absolute values. Which matters depends on how you use the output.





Slide 33 (dark): Closing loop.
Slide 34 (back to readmissions): Return to the opening hook. Now students know what to ask: baseline, precision/recall, ROC, subgroups, calibration. The operating-point choice is a cost-benefit decision, not a statistical one.
Slide 35 (takeaways): Six bullets. Reveal incrementally.
Slide 36 (next time): Forward pointers. Act 2 opens with bootstrap, which gives CIs on today’s metrics. Ch 12 does formal inference on logistic coefficients. Ch 13 adds trees.




If running short (< 60 min available): - Cut the GD convergence slide (slide 12); just mention it. - Skip the PR curve (slide 23); threshold bars (slide 24) cover the same tradeoff. - Shorten subgroup block to just the AUC bar chart (slide 29); skip boxplot.
If running long (> 80 min): - Add a worked confusion matrix exercise after slide 17: “if the threshold is 0.3, fill in the cells.” - Expand the base rate neglect exercise: have students compute for 0.1% prevalence, then 1%, then 10%, and graph P(cancer | positive) vs prevalence.





Slide 20 — FP vs FN tradeoff. Commit cards or PollEv. 2 minutes. Goal: students internalize that the tradeoff is context-dependent.
Slide 28 — which subgroup fails? Think-pair. 2 minutes. Goal: students anticipate the reveal, which makes slide 29 land harder.






Readmissions hook (opening + closing): ties statistics to a real medical operations problem. CMS HRRP penalties are real — up to 3% of Medicare reimbursements.
Framingham (throughout): 1948 start, 3 generations followed. Named after Framingham, Massachusetts. Identified cholesterol, BP, and smoking as cardiovascular risk factors.
99% accurate cancer test (base rate): a stand-in for mammography critiques — high sensitivity and specificity, low prevalence, and low positive predictive value. Real debate: 2009 USPSTF recommendation against annual mammography under 50 was driven by exactly this math.

text:

2 min think. 3 min share with a neighbor. 2 min class discussion.

title: Lecture 1: Introduction
text:
AI can build the analysis

can you trust it?



building analyses is getting easier every year
evaluating whether it’s trustworthy — that’s getting harder
MS&E graduates are in an extremely strong position here


This is the philosophical core of the course. Pause after “can you trust it?” Let it land. These three bullets should feel weighty, not rushed.

title: Lecture 1: Introduction
text:
the deal

we use AI to arrive at the best decisions we can



then we defend these conclusions, real-time, in-person
your writing should represent your authentic opinion and voice




we’re even. we’re going into this future together.


This frames the grading philosophy. Quizzes test understanding, not memorized syntax. Review sessions test whether you can explain your homework. The project presentation tests whether you can sell your analysis authentically. The skills that matter: tell a good decision from a bad one, identify the right framework, present the result.

title: Lecture 1: Introduction

2 min think. 3 min share with a neighbor. 2 min class discussion.


DISCUSSION: Think-pair-share (7 min) If stuck: “How was the data collected? What assumptions did the model make? Could there be confounders? What’s the cost of being wrong?” Key insight: This is “critical evaluation” — the central skill of the course. The answer isn’t “don’t use AI” — it’s “know what to check.”

title: Lecture 1: Introduction
text:
Excess Readmission Ratio


ERR = predicted readmissions / expected readmissions

above 1.0 → more readmissions than expected → penalty


Show this AFTER students have seen the histogram in the notebook. They’ve already formed intuitions about the distribution. Now the definition lands.

title: Lecture 1: Introduction
text:
your hospital’s ERR is 1.05. why might your readmissions be so high? what questions would you ask, or what data would you gather, to understand why — and to figure out what you might do to lower them?


2 min think. 3 min share. 2 min class.


DISCUSSION: Think-pair-share (7 min) If stuck: “What kinds of patients does your hospital serve? What happens after they’re discharged? Is the expected rate adjusted for your patient population?” Key insight: Reducing ERR requires prediction (which patients are at risk?) and intervention (what can we change?). This is not just inference — it’s a decision problem.

This is the throughline. Connect each bullet back to a montage example. “Consequential” doesn’t just mean “big” — it means the cost of being wrong is high, and someone has to live with the outcome.

Financial: Zillow lost $881M because its pricing model was wrong. Wealthfront manages $50B — a bug in tax-loss harvesting costs real people real money.
Human: COMPAS scores determine bail — wrong score, someone sits in jail who shouldn’t. Pfizer vaccine — wrong call on efficacy, millions don’t get vaccinated (or get a bad vaccine). Hospital penalties — wrong readmission estimate, a hospital serving vulnerable patients loses funding.
Irreversible: You can’t un-sentence someone. You can’t un-approve a drug. Surgical decisions, infrastructure investments — some choices are one-way doors.
Scale: WFP feeds 2M people — reallocation affects every one of them. Netflix serves 200M users — a recommendation algorithm shapes what the whole world watches.
