title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
the perils of summary statistics

four datasets. identical mean, SD, correlation, regression line.

so they must look the same, right?

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text: Let this land. “Same statistics, completely different data.” This is the single most important lesson in EDA: numbers alone are not enough. You must visualize.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
same stats, different graphs


Matejka & Fitzmaurice, “Same Stats, Different Graphs” (Autodesk Research, 2017)


The modern update to Anscombe. All 12 datasets have the same mean, SD, and correlation to two decimal places. One is a dinosaur. Spend 15 seconds — the image does the work.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text: always look at your data

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
today: the EDA workflow

first look: shape, types, first impressions
distributions: what does each variable look like?
relationships: how do variables relate?
categorical comparisons: groups and compositions
missing data: how much? any patterns?
confounding: when associations mislead


Six parts, following the book chapter exactly. Each one builds on the last. We’ll use one primary dataset — Airbnb listings — throughout.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
four modes of statistics

summary: describe the data (today)
prediction: forecast new values
inference: quantify uncertainty
causation: establish cause and effect


today we focus on summary — what’s in the data, and what traps are hiding in it

Brief framing. We’ll return to prediction, inference, and causation throughout the course. Today is about looking before you model.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text: part 1: first look at the data

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
the dataset: NYC Airbnb
29,142 listings from Inside Airbnb — every active rental in New York City

you’re a traveler trying to find a good deal. where do you start?

Set the scene. This is real data — not a cleaned textbook dataset. It has all the problems real data has.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
section: Demo: first look at Airbnb data
text:
Demo: first look at Airbnb data
open in Colab

DEMO (~5 min). Walk through chapter 2, Part 1.
Key cells to run: - df.shape → (29,142, 96) — that’s 96 columns! - Select 11 columns: name, neighbourhood_group_cleansed, neighbourhood_cleansed, room_type, price, bedrooms, beds, security_deposit, number_of_reviews, review_scores_rating, accommodates - df.dtypes → note that most are int64/float64, text columns are object - df.describe() → scan the count row: different counts for different columns (missing data!) - df.head() → first impressions
End with: “we have 29,000+ listings, 11 columns, and already some gaps in the count row.”

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
what the demo showed

29,142 rows, 96 columns → selected 11
dtypes: mix of int64, float64, object
.describe() count row: some columns have fewer entries — missing data


six semantic types: continuous, discrete, nominal, ordinal, text, identifier

The six semantic types from the book. Price is continuous, bedrooms is discrete, borough is nominal, review scores could be treated as ordinal. The key lesson: pandas dtype tells you how it’s stored, not what it means.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text: part 2: distributions

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
Demo: price distribution
open in Colab

DEMO (~5 min). Walk through Part 2 of the chapter.
Key cells to run: - Histogram of all prices → bunched at left, invisible tail - Zoom to $1–$500 → reveals the shape: peak near $100, long right tail - Before computing: ask students to estimate the mean vs. median - Show the histogram with mean (red dashed) and median (orange) marked
Key moment: “Most listings are $50–$200, but a few are $999. Does the mean represent a typical listing?”

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text: what does “typical” mean?

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
section: the histogram tells a different story
text:
the histogram tells a different story
dramatic right skew — long tail to $999/night

most listings: $50–$200
a few listings: $500+


which single number represents a “typical” listing?

This motivates the definitions that follow. Students have seen the histogram — now they need vocabulary to describe what they saw.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
mean vs. median


mean

\(\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n x_i\)

the arithmetic average
sensitive to extreme values





median

the middle value when sorted

half below, half above
barely affected by outliers



Show these AFTER the histogram. The definitions land because students already have a visual intuition for why the mean is misleading here.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
which summary do you trust?
the mean is $133. the median is $100.

if you price your listing at the mean, roughly what fraction of listings are cheaper than yours?


well over half — the right tail pulls the mean above most of the data

Give 15 seconds. The answer is well over half. This is the core lesson: when data is right-skewed, the mean overrepresents expensive listings. The median is a better measure of “typical.”

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
quartiles and IQR


quartiles

divide sorted data into four equal parts

Q1 (25th percentile), Q2 (median), Q3 (75th percentile)





interquartile range (IQR)

Q3 − Q1 — the width of the middle 50%. a robust measure of spread.

for Airbnb prices: Q1 = $67, Q3 = $165, IQR = $98

Robust = barely affected by extreme values. Mean and SD are not robust. Median and IQR are. When you have heavy tails, robust summaries are more reliable.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
same data, different lens


raw scale
histogram bunched at left, invisible tail
mean pulled far from center

log scale
roughly symmetric
structure in the tail becomes visible


use a log axis to look at skewed data; log transform to model it

The log scale doesn’t change the data — it changes the ruler. Each tick mark represents a multiplication (×10) rather than an addition (+100). The book shows three panels: linear histogram, log-axis with log-spaced bins, and log-transformed histogram.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text: part 3: relationships between variables

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
I’m about to plot price (log scale, y-axis) vs. number of guests (x-axis)

sketch what you expect
does each additional guest add a fixed dollar amount, or multiply the price?
does the spread stay constant or grow?



1 min sketch or discuss. then I’ll reveal the plot.


DISCUSSION: Predict-then-reveal (3 min) After 1 min, show the scatter plot from the demo. The key question is additive vs. multiplicative: if each guest adds $50, the relationship is linear on the raw scale but curved on the log scale. If each guest multiplies the price by 1.3×, the relationship is linear on the log scale. Most students will predict “goes up” but won’t distinguish additive from multiplicative. The reveal: it’s roughly linear on the log scale — multiplicative. The spread also increases with group size.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
Demo: scatter plots and 2D histograms
open in Colab

DEMO (~3 min). Walk through Part 3 of the chapter.
Key cells to run: - Price vs. accommodates scatter: linear scale (overplotted mess) → log scale on y-axis (clear upward trend) - 2D histogram: overplotted scatter vs. 2D histogram with log color scale - Key insight: most listings accommodate 1–4 guests at $50–$200/night
The 2D histogram is the tool for dense data — scatter plots fail when points pile up.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
when scatter plots fail
29,000 points stacked on top of each other — you can’t see the pattern

2D histogram: bin both axes, color by count

reveals that the overwhelming majority of listings are 1–4 guests at $50–$200
the expensive outliers are rare, not representative


Brief slide to anchor what the demo showed. The 2D histogram is one of the most useful and least-taught EDA tools.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text: part 4: categorical variables

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
room types and boroughs


room types

Entire home/apt: 15,022
Private room: 13,469
Shared room: 651


boroughs

5 boroughs, 212 neighborhoods
Manhattan has the most listings



bar charts make these comparisons easy — pie charts don’t

Set up the pie chart critique. The numbers come directly from the book chapter.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
section: how to lie like Steve Jobs
text:
how to lie like Steve Jobs


Steve Jobs presenting US smartphone market share. Apple has 19.5% of the market, but the 3D perspective and positioning make it look comparable to RIM’s 39%. A bar chart would instantly show the truth. Pie charts mislead because humans are bad at comparing angles and areas — especially when 3D perspective distorts them further.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
Demo: categorical comparisons
open in Colab

DEMO (~5 min). Walk through Part 4 of the chapter.
Key cells to run: - Bar chart of room type counts - Bar chart of top 10 neighborhoods - Pie chart vs. bar chart for neighborhoods (pie makes Williamsburg, Bed-Stuy, Harlem indistinguishable) - Dodged bar chart: room type by borough - Box plots: price by room type (entire homes ~2× private rooms) - Violin plots: price by borough (Manhattan has wider spread)
Key moment: the pie vs. bar comparison. “Can you tell which neighborhood has more listings — Williamsburg or Bed-Stuy? In the pie chart, they look identical. The bar chart makes it obvious.”

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
you’ve seen price distributions for the whole city

predict: do entire homes or private rooms have higher median price?
by how much? (2×? 5×? 10×?)
does the spread differ?



30 sec think. 1 min pair. then I’ll show the box plot.


DISCUSSION: Predict-then-reveal (3 min) Reveal: entire homes are roughly 2× private rooms in median price. Private room prices cluster tightly around $60; entire home prices spread from $100 to $300. The violin plot makes this especially clear — the private room violin is narrow and peaked, the entire home violin is wide and flat.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
comparing compositions
three ways to show room type by borough:

dodged bar chart → compare counts across room types
stacked bar chart → see total listings per borough
standardized bar chart → compare proportions


Manhattan has a higher proportion of entire homes than Brooklyn

The three bar chart types from the book. Each one answers a different question. The standardized chart is the one that reveals composition differences — and sets up the confounding story in Part 6.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text: what’s missing?

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
40% of security deposits are missing
11,827 out of 29,142 listings have no deposit listed

is that random, or does it mean something?

The book shows overlapping density histograms comparing prices for listings with vs. without a deposit. If the distributions look the same, missingness might be random. If they differ, the missingness is informative.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
three patterns of missing data


MCAR (missing completely at random)

missingness has no pattern — unrelated to any variable



MAR (missing at random)

missingness depends on observed variables



MNAR (missing not at random)

missingness depends on the unobserved value itself — the most dangerous pattern


Brief definitions here. Chapter 3 covers these in depth with the hospital data. For now: “MCAR is rare, MAR is manageable, MNAR introduces bias.”

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
a host lists a $500/night apartment but doesn’t fill in the security deposit field

is the deposit more likely to be high or low?
does this look like MCAR, MAR, or MNAR?
if we drop listings with missing deposits, what happens to our price statistics?



1 min think. 2 min pair. 1 min share.


DISCUSSION: Think-pair-share (4 min) Key insight: expensive listings might skip the deposit field because they handle deposits differently (through a property manager, or via Airbnb’s built-in damage protection). If high-price listings are more likely to have missing deposits, dropping them would shift our price distribution downward. This is a possible MAR pattern — missingness depends on observed price.
If a student says “maybe hosts with no deposit are more casual” — that’s also a valid hypothesis. The point is to think about why data is missing, not just that it’s missing.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
section: MNAR in the wild: hospital readmissions
text:
MNAR in the wild: hospital readmissions






CMS suppresses hospitals with “too few” readmissions to report — the value itself determines whether you see it

Brief callback to the hospital data. The left panel shows all hospitals. The right panel shows what happens when the smallest hospitals are suppressed — the reported mean shifts. This is MNAR: the count drives the suppression, and we want to analyze counts. Full treatment in Chapter 3.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text: association is not causation

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
Manhattan vs. Brooklyn
median price: Manhattan $135, Brooklyn $90

Manhattan is $45/night more expensive


but is that comparing like with like?

Set up the confounding reveal. The overall gap is $45. But what happens when we control for room type?

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
control for room type




Manhattan
Brooklyn
gap




Entire home
$180
$140
$40


Private room
$85
$62
$23


Shared room
$60
$35
$25


Overall
$135
$90
$45




the overall gap ($45) exceeds every room-type gap ($23–$40)

This is a composition effect. Manhattan has a higher proportion of entire homes (the most expensive room type), which pulls up its overall median. When comparing “like with like” — entire homes to entire homes, private rooms to private rooms — the borough difference shrinks. The overall gap overstates the true effect of location on price.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
the confounder


confounder

a variable that affects both the treatment and the outcome

inflates (or deflates) the observed association beyond the true causal effect


room type drives both location distribution and price

Manhattan isn’t as much more expensive as it looks — room type is doing some of the work

Room type is the confounder. Manhattan has more entire homes, and entire homes cost more. If you don’t control for room type, you attribute the composition difference to the borough. This is a causal concept; we’ll formalize it in Act 3. For now: “when you see an association, ask what else could drive both variables.”

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
section: students who attend office hours get higher grades
text:
students who attend office hours get higher grades

how much of the grade boost is from office hours, and how much from the kind of student who shows up?


motivation drives both OH attendance and studying — the raw correlation overstates the causal effect

A second example to reinforce the pattern. The association is real. Office hours probably do help. But the observed effect overstates the true causal benefit, because the same motivation that drives students to attend also drives them to study harder independently.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
how to lie with data

what’s wrong with each of these?

Quick warm-up. Show the two bad viz slides back-to-back, giving 20-30 seconds each. Students call out what they see. No formal activity structure — just hands up.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
y-axis is inverted — 0 at top, 1,000 at bottom


Reuters / C. Chan, 2014. Data: Florida Dept. of Law Enforcement


The red fill evokes blood dripping down — visually powerful but deeply misleading. At first glance, the downward slope after 2005 looks like a decrease. In fact, the y-axis runs from 0 (top) to 1,000 (bottom), so downward means more deaths. Gun deaths in Florida roughly doubled after Stand Your Ground.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
dates are not in chronological order, but sorted by bar height to fake a downward trend


Georgia Dept. of Public Health, May 2020


The x-axis reads: 28Apr, 27Apr, 29Apr, 01May, 30Apr, etc. This is sorted by total case count (decreasing), not by date. The visual impression is “cases are declining” — but the dates are scrambled. When plotted chronologically, cases were roughly flat or rising. Georgia DPH later corrected the chart after public outcry.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
takeaways

EDA workflow: shape → types → distributions → relationships → missing data
distributions reveal what summaries hide: always plot; use log scale for heavy tails
missing data tells a story: ask why before dropping rows
an association can be real without being causal: confounders inflate observed effects


Map each bullet back to a specific moment from the lecture. EDA checklist → the first look at Airbnb. Distributions → mean vs. median. Missing data → the 40% missing deposits. Association ≠ causation → Manhattan vs. Brooklyn, office hours.

we can describe and diagnose the data

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
am I pricing this right?


A friend just put their first Brooklyn listing on Airbnb and texts: “am I pricing this right?” First instinct — charge the average. But the average knows nothing about the listing. Their place has two bedrooms; bigger places should cost more. Before you answer, you want a pricing model that reflects what you actually know about the listing. That’s what this chapter builds, in two stages: (1) ignore every feature and predict a single number (the mean), then (2) let the prediction respond to one feature (bedrooms) by fitting a line.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
a friend’s Brooklyn listing
text from a friend: “just listed my 2-BR on Airbnb — am I pricing this right?”

first instinct: charge the average


but the average knows nothing about their listing


bigger places should cost more — can we do better?

This is the motivating scenario for the whole chapter. Stage 1: predict with no features (the mean). Stage 2: predict with one feature (bedrooms). Along the way: vectors, norms, projection, R².

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
today

vectors in data: rows, columns, norms
predicting with no features: the mean
predicting with one feature: simple regression
how good is the fit?: \(R^2\) and correlation
regression to the mean


Quick agenda. Jump straight in.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text: vectors in data

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
two views of a dataset



listing
bedrooms
bathrooms
price




1
1
1
100


2
2
1
150


3
3
2
250


4
2
2
200


5
1
1
120





row view: each listing is a point in \(\mathbb{R}^d\) — listing 3 = \((3, 2, 250)\)
column view: each feature is a vector in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) — bedrooms = \([1, 2, 3, 2, 1]\)


Two complementary views. Rows → points → similarity between listings. Columns → vectors → what a model can combine. Both matter today.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
rows as points

nearby points = similar listings

Five listings plotted in bedroom/bathroom space. Listings 1 and 5 sit almost on top of each other. Listing 3 is farthest out.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
visualizing high-dimensional vectors
a column of \(n\) prices lives in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) — too many dimensions to draw as an arrow

index plot: plot value vs listing index





black = price, red = bedrooms (sorted by price) — they move together

The index plot is how we’ll visualize n-dimensional column vectors. Sort listings by price, then plot both columns on the same axes. When two vectors rise and fall together across listings, they “point” in the same direction in R^n. This visual agreement is what the inner product will measure.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
difference vector
\[\text{listing 3} - \text{listing 1} = (3, 2) - (1, 1) = (2, 1)\]

tells us how they differ: +2 bedrooms, +1 bathroom


but how far apart as a single number?

The difference vector captures direction and magnitude of change. To collapse to a scalar, we need the norm.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
norm = length of a vector


Norm

\(\|v\| = \sqrt{v_1^2 + v_2^2 + \cdots + v_n^2}\)
Pythagorean theorem in \(n\) dimensions.


np.linalg.norm([2, 1])
# sqrt(4 + 1) = 2.24


distance = norm of the difference: \(d(u, v) = \|u - v\|\)

The norm is just a generalized Pythagorean theorem. Distance between two vectors = norm of their difference.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
section: the norm will measure prediction error
text:
the norm will measure prediction error
predict every listing with a single number \(\widehat{y}\)

residual: \(\epsilon_i = y_i - \widehat{y}\) — one error per listing







\(\|\epsilon\|\) measures total prediction error

Preview of the geometry. Vertical segments from black dots (actual prices) to the green horizontal line (a constant prediction) are the residuals. Their norm is one number measuring how wrong we are overall. Our job is to make it small.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
what does it mean for two Airbnb listings to be “similar”?



think (30s): which features should count?
pair (1 min): do you agree on the same features?
share: what’s your definition of “close”?



DISCUSSION: Think-pair-share (3 min) Prompt: “what does it mean for two listings to be similar?” If stuck: “if one is $100 and the other is $500, are they similar? what if the features match perfectly?” Key insight: “similar” depends on what you care about. Distance in feature space is one definition — but it weights every feature equally, and features with big numeric ranges dominate. This is why we’ll later normalize.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text: predicting with no features

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
the simplest predictor: a constant
\(n\) prices. what single number \(\widehat{y}\) should we guess for every listing?







Before any features, the simplest thing we can do: predict the same number for every listing. What should that number be?

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
two natural losses
minimize absolute error or squared error?






absolute → median; squared → mean

Left panel: sum of absolute deviations has V-shape, minimum at the median. Right panel: sum of squared errors is parabolic, minimum at the mean. Try the interactive widget in the book to explore this.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
why should the mean win?
before the proof, a moment of reflection:

as \(c\) moves through the data, what happens to the derivative of \((y_i - c)^2\)?


why does that force a balance exactly at \(\bar{y}\)?

Reflection prompt before the calculus. Give students 20-30 seconds to think about why squared error pulls toward the mean. Each squared term contributes a linear derivative in c — summing them gives a linear function that crosses zero at the average. This is the intuition the proof formalizes.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
section: why squared error gives the mean
text:
why squared error gives the mean
\[\frac{d}{dc}\sum_i (y_i - c)^2 = -2\sum_i (y_i - c) = 0\]

\[\Longrightarrow \quad c = \frac{1}{n}\sum_i y_i = \bar{y}\]


the mean is the optimal constant predictor under squared error

Two-line calculus proof. Derivative is linear in c, so there’s a unique zero — at the mean. Later this same argument gives us the conditional mean: within any region of x, squared-error regression predicts the average of y in that region.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
notation


Regression notation


\(y = (y_1, \ldots, y_n)\) — response vector (actual prices)
\(\widehat{y}\) — prediction vector
\(\bar{y} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_i y_i\) — sample mean
\(\epsilon = y - \widehat{y}\) — residual vector
\(\beta_0\) — intercept


for the constant model: \(\widehat{y}_i = \beta_0\), and the best \(\widehat{\beta}_0 = \bar{y}\)

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
residuals sum to zero
when \(\widehat{y}_i = \bar{y}\):
\[\sum_i \epsilon_i = \sum_i (y_i - \bar{y}) = n\bar{y} - n\bar{y} = 0\]

in vector notation:
\[\mathbf{1}^T \epsilon = 0\]


the residual is orthogonal to the ones vector — our first normal equation

Remember this pattern — it generalizes. The best prediction makes the residual orthogonal to every feature used in the model. For the constant model, the only “feature” is the ones vector.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
squared error → conditional mean
same argument, applied within each slice of \(x\):
squared-error regression estimates \(\mathbb{E}[Y \mid X = x]\)

(absolute error → conditional median; other losses → other summaries)

This is one of the most important properties of squared loss: the regression function estimates the conditional mean. The “best line” passes through the conditional means of y given x.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
when should you predict with the mean vs the median?



turn to your neighbor (2 min)
real estate prices: mean or median?
insurance claims: mean or median?
what’s the difference between “typical” and “average”?



DISCUSSION: Think-pair-share (3 min) Prompt: “mean vs median — when does each win?” If stuck: “which is sensitive to outliers? which gives the right answer for a total cost estimate?” Key insight: Median is robust to outliers and answers “what’s a typical listing?” Mean answers “if I rent out every listing, what’s the average revenue per listing?” Squared error cares about total cost — you can’t ignore the $999 listings. This is why regression uses squared error, not absolute error.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text: predicting with one feature

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
the mean ignores bedrooms

a 5-bedroom apartment and a studio get the same prediction

The mean gets the overall level right but misses the upward trend. Can we do better with bedrooms?

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
section: a linear combination of two columns
text:
a linear combination of two columns
\[\widehat{y}_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \, x_i\]

\(\widehat{y}\) is a linear combination of \(\mathbf{1}\) and \(x\):
\[\widehat{y} = \beta_0 \, \mathbf{1} + \beta_1 \, x\]








The prediction is a weighted sum of two column vectors: the ones vector (scaled by the intercept) and the bedrooms vector (scaled by the slope). Different weights give different predictions.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
the span: all reachable predictions


Span

\(\text{span}(\mathbf{1}, x) = \{\beta_0 \mathbf{1} + \beta_1 x : \beta_0, \beta_1 \in \mathbb{R}\}\)
all lines you could draw through the scatter plot


every \((\beta_0, \beta_1)\) traces a different line — which one is best?

Every choice of (β₀, β₁) traces a different line. The span of {1, x} is the set of all possible prediction vectors. Before we show candidate lines, we want students to commit to a criterion for “best.”

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
before you see any candidate lines: what would “best” look like?



think (30s): commit to a criterion
“passes through the middle of the cloud” is a starting point
make it precise enough that you could compute it



DISCUSSION: Predict-then-reveal (2 min) Prompt: “commit to a criterion for ‘best’ BEFORE seeing any lines.” If stuck: “close your left eye and imagine the vertical gaps from each point to a line.” Key insight: Most students land on ‘balances the gaps’ or ‘minimizes total error’ — that’s squared error, and it leads directly to projection. Now reveal the candidate lines.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
section: many lines are possible — which is best?
text:
many lines are possible — which is best?



red, blue: too steep at the low end
purple: slopes the wrong way
green: closer but still arbitrary



we need a principled criterion — and it turns out to be geometric

Now the reveal. Walk through why each is wrong. The point: eyeballing isn’t enough. We need a principled definition of “best” — and the geometric view (projection) will give it to us.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
switch to the column view
\(y\), \(\mathbf{1}\), and \(x\) all live in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) — one coordinate per listing

\(\text{span}\{\mathbf{1}, x\}\) = a flat plane floating in \(\mathbb{R}^n\)


every line you could draw = one point on that plane


the actual price vector \(y\) sits off the plane — no line fits every listing perfectly

Before showing the projection cartoon, get students to picture the column view. n listings → vectors in R^n. Span of two columns = a 2D plane inside that high-dimensional space. Every regression line is a point on the plane. The data vector y lives off the plane. Now the question becomes: which point on the plane is closest to y?

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
the geometric view: projection


\(y\) is a point in \(\mathbb{R}^n\)
\(\text{span}\{\mathbf{1}, x\}\) is a plane through the origin
the closest point in the plane is the best prediction
the residual \(\epsilon\) is perpendicular to the plane


Here’s the geometric heart. y is a point. The span of the features is a plane. The prediction ŷ lives in the plane — it’s a linear combination of the columns. The best ŷ is the point in the plane closest to y. Shortest distance from a point to a plane = perpendicular. That perpendicularity is the defining property of least squares.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
the inner product


Inner product (dot product)

\(u^T v = \sum_{i=1}^n u_i v_i\)

positive → same direction
zero → orthogonal
negative → opposite directions



take \(u = (1, 0)\) along the x-axis:

\(u \cdot (1, 1) = 1\) → acute angle
\(u \cdot (0, 1) = 0\) → right angle
\(u \cdot (-1, 0) = -1\) → opposite



price and bedrooms both trend up → large positive \(y^T x\)

The inner product makes “perpendicular” precise. Zero inner product = orthogonal. The small 2D examples ground the rule: sign of the inner product tracks the angle. Callback to the index plot: if two columns rise and fall together, their inner product is positive and large.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
orthogonality = best fit
the best prediction makes the residual orthogonal to every feature:
\[\mathbf{1}^T \epsilon = 0 \qquad \text{and} \qquad x^T \epsilon = 0\]


first: residuals sum to zero (same as the mean model!)
second: residuals have no remaining linear pattern with \(x\)



if some feature still aligned with the residual, you could reduce error by adjusting its coefficient

Two orthogonality conditions pin down the unique best line. The principle is the same as for the mean, just one more condition. If x^T ε were nonzero, we could absorb some of that pattern into the slope — so at the optimum, it must be zero.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
normal equations


Normal equations (simple regression)

\(\mathbf{1}^T \epsilon = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad x^T \epsilon = 0\)
where \(\epsilon = y - \beta_0 \mathbf{1} - \beta_1 x\)
— the algebraic form of “residual orthogonal to features”

these two equations determine the unique \((\widehat{\beta}_0, \widehat{\beta}_1)\)

Two equations, two unknowns. The normal equations are just the orthogonality conditions, written out. Every regression problem reduces to solving a system like this.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
the projection picture

\(\widehat{y}\) = orthogonal projection of \(y\) onto \(\text{span}\{\mathbf{1}, x\}\)
the right angle is why it’s the best fit

Clean version of the diagram. Perpendicular = shortest. This is the defining property of least squares.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
simple regression on Airbnb
model = LinearRegression()
model.fit(df[['bedrooms']], prices)

28,778 listings


ŷ = 71 + 49 × bedrooms
R² = 0.156


\[\widehat{y} = 71 + 49 \times \text{bedrooms}\]

The full NYC Airbnb dataset, filtered to prices in [$10, $500]. One feature, closed-form solution via the normal equations. R² = 0.156 — bedrooms alone explain about 16% of the variation.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
interpreting the coefficients
\[\widehat{y} = 71 + 49 \times \text{bedrooms}\]


intercept $71: predicted price for a studio (0 bedrooms)
slope $49: each additional bedroom → $49 more per night



association, not causation — larger listings differ in many ways

The intercept is the base price of a studio. The slope says each additional bedroom is associated with $49 more per night. But bigger apartments differ in many ways from smaller ones — location, amenities, quality. We can’t say “adding a bedroom causes $49.” We’ll revisit this seriously in Chapter 18.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
plug in a few listings
\[\widehat{y} = 71 + 49 \times \text{bedrooms}\]




listing
prediction




studio
$71


1-bedroom
$120


3-bedroom
$217


5-bedroom
$314





back to our friend’s 2-BR: model predicts $169/night

Concrete numbers make the model tangible. Plug bedrooms into the formula and read off predictions. For the 2-BR: 71 + 49 × 2 = 169. Our friend now has a starting anchor — though we’ll refine it with more features in Ch 5.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
verify orthogonality
y_hat = model.predict(df[['bedrooms']])
residuals = prices - y_hat
np.dot(residuals, np.ones(len(df)))     # ≈ 0
np.dot(residuals, df['bedrooms'].values) # ≈ 0

residuals · 1       :  -0.0000  ✓
residuals · bedrooms:  -0.0000  ✓


the errors have no linear pattern left that bedrooms could capture

Both dot products are effectively zero — the normal equations are satisfied. This is guaranteed by the math. What is NOT guaranteed is that residuals look like random noise — we’ll check that with residual plots in Chapter 5.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
what would a curved pattern in the residual plot mean?



think (30s): if residuals vs bedrooms showed a U-shape…
pair (1 min): what’s the model missing?
share: how would you fix it?



DISCUSSION: Think-pair-share (3 min) Prompt: “what does a curved residual plot tell us?” If stuck: “the orthogonality condition says residuals are uncorrelated with bedrooms linearly. What kind of pattern would still satisfy that?” Key insight: A U-shape would mean the true relationship is nonlinear — a quadratic term would help. Forward pointer to feature engineering (Ch 5): we can add bedrooms² as a new column and the same orthogonality machinery still works.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text: how good is the fit?

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
\(R^2\): fraction of variance explained


\(R^2\) (coefficient of determination)

\[R^2 = 1 - \frac{\|\epsilon\|^2}{\|y - \bar{y}\|^2}\]

\(R^2 = 0\): no better than predicting \(\bar{y}\)
\(R^2 = 1\): perfect fit



for our Airbnb regression: \(R^2 = 0.156\)

R² compares the model’s error to the baseline of always predicting the mean. Bedrooms alone explain about 16% of the price variation — the other 84% will need more features.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
correlation


Correlation (Pearson’s \(r\))

\(r(u, v) = \dfrac{(u - \bar u)^T(v - \bar v)}{\|u - \bar u\|\,\|v - \bar v\|}\)
inner product of centered, length-normalized vectors.



\(-1 \le r \le +1\)
\(r = 0\) → centered vectors are orthogonal
unitless: rescaling \(u\) or \(v\) doesn’t change \(r\)


Center each vector (subtract its mean), normalize to unit length, take the inner product. That’s correlation. It measures the angle between the centered vectors — pure direction, no units. Rescaling or shifting either input leaves r unchanged.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
\(R^2 = r(y, \widehat{y})^2\)

Pythagorean theorem (centered \(y\)):
\[\|y - \bar{y}\|^2 = \|\widehat{y} - \bar{y}\|^2 + \|\epsilon\|^2\]

\[R^2 = \frac{\|\widehat{y} - \bar{y}\|^2}{\|y - \bar{y}\|^2} = r(y, \widehat y)^2\]
the first ratio is the correlation between \(y\) and \(\widehat{y}\), by definition

After centering, y, y-hat, and the residual form a right triangle with y as the hypotenuse. R² is the squared ratio of the leg (centered y-hat) to the hypotenuse (centered y) — and that ratio is exactly the correlation r(y, y-hat) between centered y and centered y-hat, by definition. R² measures how much of y lives in the feature span.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
\(R^2 = r^2\) in simple regression
r = df['price'].corr(df['bedrooms'])
# r = 0.3944
model.score(df[['bedrooms']], prices)
# R² = 0.1556 = r²

for one predictor: \(R^2\) is literally \(r\) squared
(with multiple predictors, only \(R^2\) applies)

0.3944² ≈ 0.1556. R² = r² is only true for simple regression. With multiple predictors there’s no single r to square — we have to compute R² directly.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text: regression to the mean

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
Galton’s heights
Francis Galton, 1886: tall parents → tall children, but less tall






934 children, 205 families: \(r \approx 0.50\), slope \(\approx 0.71\) (Galton’s own estimate: 2/3)

Galton’s actual 1886 dataset — 934 adult children from 205 families. He multiplied female heights by 1.08 to put everyone on a common male scale, then plotted each child against the “midparent height” (average of father and 1.08 × mother). The regression line is flatter than the 45-degree line (child = midparent). Parents 2 SD above average have children predicted to be only about r × 2 SD above average — pulled toward the mean. Galton himself estimated the slope at about 2/3, which is close to what we find. This is a purely statistical phenomenon, not biological. For the full story, see Salsburg, The Lady Tasting Tea, Ch. 4.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
why “regression”?
since \(|r| < 1\), predicted \(y\) is always closer to \(\bar{y}\) (in SDs) than \(x\) is to \(\bar{x}\)

Galton called this “regression toward mediocrity” — the name stuck


purely statistical, not biological or causal

The phenomenon comes from imperfect correlation, not from any physical force pulling things toward the average. Extreme values are partly signal, partly luck — and luck doesn’t persist.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
where else do you see regression to the mean?



a team wins 90% of games one season. next year?
a student scores in the 99th percentile. next exam?
a player scores 45 points (season avg: 25). next game?

why does this keep happening?


DISCUSSION: Predict-then-reveal (3 min) Prompt: “regression to the mean in the wild” If stuck: “how much of an extreme outcome is skill vs luck?” Key insight: Any time performance is skill + noise, the best predictor after an extreme event is somewhere between the extreme and the long-run average. Coaches who bench a player after one bad game, or overpay after one great game, are ignoring this. The “Sports Illustrated jinx” is regression to the mean, not a curse.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
skill + luck, luck resets
90% win rate = part skill, part luck

next season: the skill carries over, the luck resets


99th percentile exam = real knowledge + a run of favorable questions


knowledge holds; favorable run does not


whenever \(|r| < 1\), extremes on one measurement look less extreme on the next

The mechanism in every case is the same: observed performance = underlying ability + noise. The ability carries over; the noise doesn’t. Not “the team got worse” — just that an unusual streak has no reason to persist. Same logic for exam scores, stock picks, player performance, business quarters. This is not a curse, not a jinx — just algebra.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
key takeaways

two views of data: rows = points, columns = vectors in \(\mathbb{R}^n\)
the mean is the best constant predictor under squared error
simple regression is the orthogonal projection of \(y\) onto \(\text{span}\{\mathbf{1}, x\}\)
residual ⊥ features: the defining property of least squares
\(R^2 = r(y, \widehat{y})^2\): how much of \(y\) lives in the feature span
regression to the mean: extremes don’t persist


The conceptual chain: vectors → mean → simple regression → projection → R². Each step adds one feature and one orthogonality condition.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
what we can’t answer yet
\[\widehat{y} = 71 + 49 \times \text{bedrooms} \qquad R^2 = 0.16\]


what about bathrooms? room type? neighborhood?
can more features make \(R^2\) bigger?
how do we encode categorical features?



next time: multiple regression (Chapter 5)

Forward pointer. Same orthogonality principle scales to any number of features. More columns → bigger span → closer projection. We’ll also transform raw data into features a linear model can use.

title: Lecture 1 Script: Introduction to Applied Statistics
text:
Target time: 75 minutes (50-min lecture + 25 min discussion spread across)



Advance through all 10 vignettes at a steady pace — roughly 30 seconds each. Don’t explain. Let the questions land. The point is range: students should feel the breadth of what statistics touches before hearing a single definition.


Background. The Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) runs the Hospital Readmissions Reduction Program. Since 2012, hospitals with “too many” patients returning within 30 days of discharge lose up to 3% of their Medicare payments — $500K–$1M/year for a large hospital. In 2023, 2,217 hospitals were penalized.
Data. CMS publishes the Excess Readmission Ratio (ERR) for every hospital, for six conditions (heart attack, heart failure, pneumonia, hip/knee replacement, COPD, CABG). ERR is risk-adjusted: it compares a hospital’s predicted readmissions (based on its actual patients) to the expected number (based on a national model of similar patients). ERR > 1.0 triggers a penalty.
Decision. Imagine you’re an administrateoWhich hospitals should be fined? Should a hospital with ERR = 1.05 be penalized the same as one with ERR = 1.30? What if a hospital serves a disproportionately sick populatio - how should the risk adjustment account for that?
Statistics challenge. Estimation under structured missingness. Small and rural hospitals are coded “Too Few to Report” — their data is systematically suppressed, not randomly absent. Any analysis that drops these rows is biased toward large urban hospitals. The ERR itself is a ratio estimator: how much uncertainty surrounds a ratio when the denominator is modeled? This is a hypothesis testing problem (is ERR significantly > 1.0?) layered on an estimation problem (is the risk adjustment correct?).




Background. The Houston Rockets under GM Daryl Morey were among the first NBA teams to use analytics systematically. By 2017–18, the Rockets had virtually eliminated the mid-range two-pointer from their offense — the “Moreyball” thesis was that mid-range twos are the worst shot in basketball: too far for a high-percentage two, too close for a three.
Data. NBA play-by-play logs record every shot attempt: location (x, y coordinates on the court), outcome (make/miss), shot type, game context (score, time, defender distance). Kirk Goldsberry’s shot charts visualize these as heatmaps.
Decision. Should a team stop shooting mid-range twos entirely? At what expected-value threshold should a shot type be abandoned? How do you account for the fact that defenses adjust — if you never shoot from mid-range, defenders sag off and contest your threes?
Statistics challenge. Conditional expected value and selection bias. The expected points per shot depends on shot location, but also on game state, fatigue, and defender positioning. A team that only shoots threes when wide open will have a higher three-point percentage than one that shoots contested threes — comparing raw percentages is misleading. This is a problem of estimating conditional distributions: E[points | location, context].




Background. Wealthfront (and similar robo-advisors) manage ~$50B in assets. One key service: tax-loss harvesting — selling losing investments to realize capital losses that offset gains, reducing the client’s tax bill. The IRS wash-sale rule forbids repurchasing a “substantially identical” security within 30 days, so the algorithm must find substitute investments that maintain the portfolio’s risk profile while capturing the tax benefit.
Data. Daily returns for thousands of securities, client portfolio holdings, tax lots with cost basis and purchase dates, and a correlation/covariance matrix of asset returns.
Decision. For each client on each day: which lots should be sold? What substitute should be bought? How much tax savings does the harvest generate, net of transaction costs and tracking error?
Statistics challenge. Optimization under estimated covariance. The portfolio’s risk is characterized by its covariance matrix — but covariance matrices estimated from historical returns are noisy, especially when the number of assets exceeds the number of time periods. Eigenvalues are systematically biased (the largest are too large, the smallest too small). Regularization, shrinkage estimators, and factor models all address this. The decision is also sequential: harvesting today affects what you can harvest tomorrow.




Background. The World Food Programme feeds ~160 million people annually. In Yemen during the 2018–19 famine, WFP faced a resource allocation problem: fixed budget, varying need across regions, and supply chain constraints (ports, roads, warehouses). The Zero Hunger Lab at Tilburg partnered with WFP to optimize allocations.
Data. WFP’s HungerMap LIVE aggregates food security indicators — food consumption scores, dietary diversity, coping strategies — from phone surveys, satellite imagery, and market price data across ~90 countries in near real-time.
Decision. How to reallocate food baskets across regions in Yemen to feed 2 million more people at the same cost. Which regions get more? Which get less? How do you handle the political and ethical dimension of taking food from one region to give to another?
Statistics challenge. Prediction under distribution shift and optimization with uncertainty. The hunger indicators are estimated from surveys with limited sample sizes — the uncertainty varies by region. The optimization must be robust: a misallocation doesn’t just waste money, it means people go hungry. This combines linear programming (resource allocation) with statistical estimation (how hungry is each region, really?).




Background. NextEra Energy (the world’s largest generator of wind and solar energy) was siting 4.5 GW of new solar capacity across four states. Each potential parcel has different solar irradiance, land cost, grid interconnection cost, and permitting complexity.
Data. NREL’s National Solar Radiation Database provides 30+ years of hourly solar irradiance data at 4 km × 4 km resolution across the U.S. — a public dataset built from satellite imagery and ground stations. Supplemented by land price data, grid capacity maps, and environmental impact assessments.
Decision. Which parcels maximize energy production per dollar invested, subject to grid interconnection constraints and environmental review timelines? How much capacity to build in each state given regulatory uncertainty?
Statistics challenge. Spatial prediction and feature engineering. Solar irradiance varies by location, time of day, season, and weather patterns. Predicting annual energy yield from historical data requires modeling temporal correlation (cloudy days cluster) and spatial correlation (nearby parcels have similar irradiance). Feature engineering — transforming raw irradiance into useful predictors — determines whether the regression model is accurate.




Background. Pfizer’s Phase III COVID-19 vaccine trial enrolled 43,548 participants in summer 2020. Half received the vaccine, half a placebo. By November, an interim analysis found 8 cases in the vaccine group vs. 162 in the placebo group — 95% efficacy. The FDA’s Emergency Use Authorization (EUA) hinged on this analysis.
Data. A randomized controlled trial: binary treatment assignment, binary outcome (COVID infection within a monitoring window), with demographic covariates (age, sex, race, comorbidities). The Kaplan-Meier curve shows event-free survival over time.
Decision. Is 8 vs. 162 enough evidence to authorize a vaccine for hundreds of millions of people? The interim analysis was pre-planned — the trial could have continued to collect more data, but every week of delay meant thousands more infections. How do you balance statistical certainty against the cost of waiting?
Statistics challenge. Hypothesis testing with early stopping. The null hypothesis: the vaccine has no effect (efficacy = 0). Under the null, the 170 total cases should split roughly 85–85. Observing an 8–162 split is astronomically unlikely under the null (p < 10⁻³⁰). But the trial used a group sequential design with pre-specified interim analyses — if you peek at the data multiple times, your false positive rate inflates (the multiple testing problem). The O’Brien-Fleming spending function controls this by requiring more extreme results at early looks.




Background. In North Carolina’s 2016 congressional elections, Republican candidates won 53% of the statewide vote but captured 10 of 13 seats (77%). Plaintiffs in Common Cause v. Rucho argued this was evidence of partisan gerrymandering. Mathematicians at Duke (Jonathan Mattingly’s group) contributed expert testimony.
Data. Precinct-level vote totals for the 2016 election, plus the geometric shapes of the 13 congressional districts. Mattingly’s team generated 24,000+ alternative redistricting plans that satisfied all legal requirements (contiguity, equal population, Voting Rights Act compliance) but were otherwise random.
Decision. Was the 10–3 outcome evidence of intentional gerrymandering, or could it have arisen from legitimate geographic constraints (e.g., Democrats clustering in cities)? The Supreme Court ultimately ruled that partisan gerrymandering claims are non-justiciable — but the statistical methodology remains influential.
Statistics challenge. Permutation testing and simulation-based inference. Of 24,000+ legally valid random plans, essentially none produced a 10–3 outcome. The observed plan was a clear outlier. This is a permutation test: define the null distribution (random valid plans), compute a test statistic (seat share), and ask where the observed statistic falls. The challenge is that “random redistricting plan” is a complex combinatorial object — sampling from this space requires MCMC methods.




Background. In 2021, Zillow expanded its iBuying program (Zillow Offers), purchasing homes directly from sellers using its Zestimate algorithm to set prices. In Q3 2021 alone, Zillow bought 9,790 homes. By November, the company announced an $881M write-down and shut down the program entirely — it had systematically overpaid.
Data. Zillow’s Zestimate model used public records (tax assessments, sale prices, square footage), listing features (photos, descriptions), and local market data. The training data was historical sales — but in a rapidly shifting market, historical patterns broke down.
Decision. For each home: should we buy it at this price? The model’s point estimate said yes. But the model didn’t adequately account for its own uncertainty — or for the fact that Zillow’s own purchases were moving the market (reflexivity).
Statistics challenge. Prediction intervals, distribution shift, and feedback loops. A point estimate (“this home is worth $400K”) is useless without a prediction interval (“… plus or minus $30K”). If you systematically buy at the upper end of your confidence interval, you’ll overpay on average. Worse, the training data (pre-2020 sales) didn’t represent the post-COVID market (distribution shift). And the model’s own behavior changed the data distribution — buying aggressively in a market inflates prices, making the model’s training data less representative. This is a feedback loop: the model’s predictions alter the data it learns from.




Background. Broward County, Florida uses COMPAS (Correctional Offender Management Profiling for Alternative Sanctions) risk scores to inform bail and sentencing decisions. In 2016, ProPublica’s “Machine Bias” investigation found that Black defendants who did not reoffend were nearly twice as likely to be flagged as high-risk (false positive rate: 45% vs. 23% for white defendants).
Data. ProPublica obtained COMPAS scores and two-year recidivism outcomes for ~7,000 defendants in Broward County. Features included age, sex, race, prior convictions, charge degree, and the COMPAS risk score (1–10).
Decision. Should a judge set bail at $500 or $50,000? The COMPAS score is one input, but it carries the weight of algorithmic authority. If the algorithm is racially biased, it systematically affects who sits in jail pretrial — with cascading effects on employment, housing, and family stability.
Statistics challenge. Fairness in classification under base rate differences. Northpointe (COMPAS’s maker) responded that their algorithm was calibrated: among defendants scored as high-risk, the recidivism rate was similar across races (~60%). Both ProPublica and Northpointe were correct — but they measured fairness differently. It is mathematically impossible to simultaneously satisfy calibration (equal predictive value across groups) and equal false positive/negative rates when the base rates differ (Chouldechova 2017, Kleinberg-Mullainathan-Raghavan 2016). This is a fundamental impossibility theorem, not a fixable bug. The decision of which fairness criterion to use is a values question, not a statistical one.




Background. In 2006, Netflix offered a $1M prize to anyone who could improve its recommendation algorithm (Cinematch) by 10% on RMSE. The contest ran for three years and attracted 40,000+ teams. The winning solution used an ensemble of matrix factorization methods. Netflix estimated that better recommendations could save $1B/year in reduced churn — 80% of viewing comes from recommendations.
Data. 100 million ratings (1–5 stars) from 480,000 users on 17,000 movies. The matrix is ~99% empty — most users haven’t rated most movies. The task: predict the missing entries.
Decision. Which movies should Netflix recommend to which users? A bad recommendation wastes the user’s time and erodes trust. At scale (200M subscribers), even small improvements in prediction accuracy translate to millions of retained subscriptions.
Statistics challenge. Matrix completion via low-rank approximation. The key insight: the user-movie matrix is approximately low-rank. A few latent factors (action preference, indie taste, recency bias) explain most of the variation. SVD and its variants decompose the observed ratings into user factors and movie factors. The challenge: the matrix is mostly missing, so you can’t just run SVD — you need optimization methods that work with incomplete data. Regularization prevents overfitting to the sparse observations. This is PCA/dimensionality reduction applied to the largest-scale collaborative filtering problem of its era.





Pause. Let this land. “Every one of those examples had three things in common: data that was messy, a model that could be wrong, and a decision that someone had to live with.”




Keep it personal and fast. The point isn’t your CV — it’s that you’ve worked on problems like the ones in the montage. Touch 3–4 of the applied roles and connect each to a vignette: “I worked at the World Food Programme on exactly the kind of allocation problem you just saw.” “The Obama campaign was a massive exercise in prediction — who’s persuadable, where do we send volunteers.”




Reinforce the throughline. “Finance, tech, energy, health, politics, logistics — the methods are the same. The data changes. The decisions change. But the structure is always: data, model, decision.”




Live poll. Show PollEv results. React to them: “Look at this room — finance, tech, healthcare, policy. The same tools work in every backyard.”




Prompt: “What kinds of consequential decisions do you expect to make in your career? What data will you have? What uncertainties will you face?”
Think-pair-share: 2 min think, 3 min share with neighbor, 2 min class discussion.
Facilitation notes. This is a warm-up. Students should connect the montage to their own futures. If energy is low, cold-call: “You said finance — give me one decision a portfolio manager makes where the data is messy.” The key realization: whatever domain they care about, the structure is data → model → decision.




This is the philosophical core. Pause after “can you trust it?” — let it land.
“Here’s the thing about 2026. Building an analysis is easy. You can hand a dataset to an AI and get a polished report in 30 seconds. The question is: should you believe it? Can you spot the errors? Can you tell when the conclusions don’t follow from the evidence?”
“That’s what this course teaches. Not how to build analyses — you can already do that. How to evaluate them. How to know when the model is wrong, when the data is biased, when the conclusion is fragile.”
“MS&E graduates are in an extremely strong position here. You understand optimization, decision-making, systems. Add statistical rigor, and you can be the person in the room who catches the mistake before it costs $881 million.”




“Here’s the social contract for this course. We use AI. Both sides. I use AI to build course materials. You use AI on your homework. That’s fine.”
“But then you defend your conclusions. In person. In review sessions. In quizzes. In the project presentation. Your writing should represent your authentic opinion — not a generated essay you didn’t read.”




Prompt: “An AI hands you an analysis that says Hospital X should be fined. What questions do you ask before signing off?”
Think-pair-share: 2 min think, 3 min share, 2 min class.
Facilitation notes. If stuck, prompt: “How was the data collected? What assumptions did the model make? What’s the cost of being wrong — both of fining a good hospital and of letting a bad one off?” The key insight: critical evaluation is the central skill. The answer isn’t “don’t use AI” — it’s “know what to check.”






Switch to pre-filled Jupyter notebook. Walk through: 1. pd.read_csv(...) — load CMS data 2. .head() — what does the raw data look like? 3. .shape — how big is it? 4. Histogram of ERR — notice it’s centered near 1.0, with real spread
Show the ERR definition after students have seen the histogram — they’ve already formed intuitions about the distribution.



Prompt: “Your hospital’s ERR is 1.05. Why might your readmissions be so high? What questions would you ask, or what data would you gather, to understand why — and what you might do to lower them?”
Think-pair-share: 2 min think, 3 min share, 2 min class.
Facilitation notes. Push toward: “What kinds of patients does your hospital serve? What happens after discharge? Is the expected rate adjusted for your patient population?” Key insight: Reducing ERR requires prediction (which patients are at risk?) and intervention (what can we change?). This is not just inference — it’s a decision problem.



Back to notebook: .isna().sum(). Point out the “Too Few to Report” pattern. “This is missingness that’s not random — it’s systematically missing for small and rural hospitals. Any analysis that drops these rows is biased. We’ll formalize this concept later.”



Prompt: “Which patients are most likely to be in the ‘too few’ category? What does that mean for fairness?”
Think-pair-share: 2 min think, 3 min share, 2 min class.
Facilitation notes. Think about which hospitals have small sample sizes: rural, specialty, serving underrepresented populations. Data that’s missing is not random — it’s systematically missing for the most vulnerable patients. Any analysis that ignores this is biased.





Show the four-modes table. Walk through each row using the hospital data:

Summary: “We just did this — loaded the data, plotted the ERR distribution. Summary tells you what the data looks like.”
Prediction: “Can we predict which hospitals will be flagged? Given a hospital’s patient mix and staffing, what ERR should we expect? That’s Act 1.”
Inference: “Your hospital’s ERR is 1.05. Is that real or noise? Should you be fined based on that number? That’s Act 2.”
Causation: “Does the fine actually work? Do penalized hospitals improve — or do they just game the metrics? That’s Act 3.”

Key line: “Same data. Four completely different questions. Each one requires different tools.”






Walk through quickly. The three acts are how the course unfolds — each one builds on the last: - Act I: Build Models — explore, clean, predict (regression, trees, features, Lectures 1–7) - Act II: Trust Models — sample, test, infer (bootstrap, hypothesis tests, Lectures 8–12) - Act III: See Further — classify, cluster (PCA, kmeans), cause (causal inference, Lectures 13–19)



Point to the table connecting each montage vignette to specific topics and acts. “By the end of the quarter, you’ll have the tools to answer all 10 of these questions.”





Keep this brisk. Point to the website for details.

Grading: HW 10%, review sessions 15%, quizzes 25%, project 30%, final 20%
AI policy: “Use it. Then defend your conclusions in person.”
Quizzes vs. final: You can skip one. Take both? We use the higher score. No risk to trying.
Device policy: Laptops in back row only. “Research shows your screen distracts the person behind you. Boredom is productive”







“Other names for this: applied statistics, data science, operations research, management science, AI/ML. I prefer: making consequential decisions with data.”



Financial ($881M), human (bail, vaccines, hospital penalties), irreversible (can’t un-sentence), scale (2M fed, 200M recommendations). “Every example in the montage is a consequential decision.”





Plant seeds — don’t define formally. “You’ll hear these words every week.” - Prediction: what will happen? - Inference: what is true about the population? - Causality: what would happen if we intervened? - Uncertainty: how confident should we be?




Show the montage grid. “We started with 10 questions. By June, you’ll be able to answer every one.”
Before-next-class checklist: read Chapter 1, install Python, sign up for Ed Discussion.
One-minute feedback form: “What was the most useful thing? What was the most confusing?”
